summary: Cathy O'Neil welcomes the controversial Facebook mood manipulation study as a valuable teaching opportunity, noting it exemplifies the ethical and methodological problems inherent in big data experimentation. She points readers to critical analyses of the study while acknowledging the irony that such experiments happen constantly at tech companies—we just rarely get to see the evidence. Her core observation is that the scandal isn't that Facebook ran this experiment, but that it became public. She worries the backlash will simply drive such research further underground rather than stopping it.
key_points: The Facebook mood study serves as a perfect case study combining ethical dilemmas, flawed methodology, and sociological implications
Experiments like this happen daily at Facebook and Google—the unusual part is public visibility into the data
The likely corporate response to criticism will be to hide future experiments, not to stop conducting them
The study has generated valuable critical analysis worth studying, including pieces on research methods and engineering of public emotion
money_quotes: What's special about this experiment isn't that it happened, but that we get to see the data.
The response to the critiques might be, sadly, that we never get another chance like this, so we have to grab the opportunity while we can.
I know this kind of experiment happens on a daily basis at a place like Facebook or Google.
It's got everything a case study should have: ethical dilemmas, questionable methodology, sociological implications, and questionable claims.
key_insight: The real scandal of the Facebook mood study isn't that tech companies manipulate users experimentally—that's routine—it's that this time we caught a glimpse of it.
